question: Vacuum quality is described by pressure in pascals or millibar. Which set of ranges correctly classifies the standard vacuum regimes used in AMO experiments?

options: UHV is defined as any pressure below $10^{-3}$ mbar; XHV starts at $10^{-7}$ mbar
Atmospheric pressure is $10^5$ Pa and UHV starts at $10^{-5}$ Pa; XHV is conventionally reserved for $< 10^{-6}$ Pa
passive.explanation: Conversions: 1 atm = $1.013 \times 10^5$ Pa = 1013 mbar = 760 Torr = 760 mmHg. AMO experiments: MOT loading uses background Rb vapour at $10^{-7}{-}10^{-9}$ mbar (HV); for long trap lifetimes (> 1 s), UHV $< 10^{-10}$ mbar is required. At $10^{-10}$ mbar, the mean free path $\lambda_{\rm mfp} \approx 500$ km — the vacuum contains $\sim 2.7 \times 10^{12}$ molecules/m$^3$ (vs $\sim 2.7 \times 10^{25}$/m$^3$ at 1 atm).

active.prompt: Match each vacuum regime to its typical application:

pairs.definition: Rotary/scroll pump output; fore-vacuum for turbo pumps; initial chamber pump-down
Electron microscopes, CVD deposition, Rb MOTs with short trap lifetime
Long-lifetime atom traps (ODT, BEC), surface science, ion traps
Space simulators, storage rings, Sr/Yb optical clocks, proton radius experiments

active.explanation: Background gas collisions are the dominant trap loss mechanism in most AMO experiments. At $p = 10^{-9}$ mbar, the collision rate for a trapped Rb atom is $\sim 1$ s$^{-1}$ — setting the minimum evaporation ramp speed. At $10^{-11}$ mbar: trap lifetime $> 100$ s, enabling long evaporation ramps and quantum simulation experiments.

question: The mean free path $\lambda_{\rm mfp}$ of a gas molecule at pressure $p$ and temperature $T$ is given below. What is the consequence at UHV for gas flow?

options: At $p \sim 10^{-9}$ mbar, $\lambda_{\rm mfp} \sim 50{-}500$ km — much larger than any chamber dimension; flow is molecular and pumping speed is set by conductance, not pump capacity
At UHV the gas is dense enough that molecule-molecule collisions still dominate; pumping speed scales linearly with pump capacity
Mean free path is independent of pressure; the flow regime depends only on chamber geometry
At rough vacuum, $\lambda_{\rm mfp}$ already exceeds the chamber dimensions, putting the flow in the Knudsen regime
passive.explanation: Flow regimes: (1) Viscous flow ($\lambda_{\rm mfp} \ll$ chamber size): molecule-molecule collisions dominate; pumping works by viscous drag. (2) Transition flow. (3) Molecular flow ($\lambda_{\rm mfp} \gg$ chamber size): molecules collide only with walls; conductance is geometry-determined. AMO chambers always operate in molecular flow regime at final pressures. Knudsen number $K_n = \lambda_{\rm mfp}/L$ characterises the regime: $K_n \gg 1$ → molecular flow.

question: Vacuum gauges measure pressure over different ranges using different physics. Which set of gauge types and ranges is correct for a typical AMO lab?

options: Pirani (thermal conductivity) $10^{-4}{-}10$ mbar; Bayard-Alpert (hot-filament ionisation) $10^{-4}{-}10^{-11}$ mbar; Penning (cold-cathode) $10^{-3}{-}10^{-11}$ mbar; RGA (quadrupole) for partial pressures
A single ion gauge can read from atmospheric pressure down to $10^{-12}$ mbar — Pirani and Penning are obsolete
Pirani gauges work by ionising residual gas; Bayard-Alpert gauges work by thermal conductivity of the gas
BA gauges cannot be used below $10^{-8}$ mbar because the X-ray-induced photocurrent floor sits at $10^{-8}$ mbar equivalent
passive.explanation: Operating principles: Pirani — heated filament; thermal conductivity of gas removes heat (pressure-dependent cooling). BA gauge — hot tungsten filament emits electrons; electrons ionise residual gas; ion current $\propto$ pressure. BA lower limit $\sim 10^{-11}$ mbar from X-ray-induced photocurrent. Extractor gauges (inverted BA) reach $10^{-12}$ mbar. RGA — quadrupole mass spectrometer for partial pressures of each species; essential for troubleshooting leaks.

active.prompt: Match each vacuum gauge to its working principle and operating range:

pairs.definition: Heated filament — heat loss via gas thermal conductivity; range $10^{-4}$ to 10 mbar
Electron ionisation of residual gas; ion current ∝ pressure; range $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-11}$ mbar
Cold cathode — magnetic field sustains plasma discharge at low pressure; robust, no filament; range $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-11}$ mbar
Quadrupole mass filter — partial pressure of each gas species by mass; identifies contamination sources

active.explanation: Key pitfalls: (1) Never expose a BA gauge filament to atmospheric pressure (burns out). (2) Ion gauges must be degassed after exposure to air. (3) Penning gauges can underestimate pressure and may not ignite below $10^{-8}$ mbar without an ionisation source. (4) RGA must be UHV-compatible (its own pump or differential pumping).

question: Vacuum pumps remove gas with pumping speed $S$ (L/s). Which combination correctly identifies the main pump types and the role each plays in a UHV chain?

options: Rotary/scroll (rough, backs turbo); turbomolecular (HV/UHV, momentum transfer in molecular flow); ion (UHV, ionises gas and buries it); NEG (chemisorption after thermal activation); TSP (Ti film chemisorbs reactive gases)
A single turbomolecular pump achieves XHV on its own; ion pumps and NEGs are redundant in well-designed systems
Ion pumps work by mechanical compression of gas like a piston; NEGs require continuous power to maintain pumping
Turbo pumps work in viscous flow; rotary pumps and NEG operate in molecular flow; TSP and ion pumps are functionally interchangeable
passive.explanation: Typical pump sequence: (1) Rotary/scroll to 10 mbar (rough). (2) Turbomolecular to $10^{-7}$ mbar (with bakeout: $10^{-9}$ mbar). (3) Ion pump activated at $< 5\times 10^{-8}$ mbar. (4) NEG activated by heating to 350–450°C — after cooling, it pumps reactive gases (H$_2$O, N$_2$, CO, CO$_2$) with no power. (5) TSP provides bursts of high pumping speed. At UHV, H$_2$ typically dominates the residual gas (outgasses from stainless steel walls).

question: The conductance $C$ of a vacuum component in molecular flow limits the effective pumping speed at the chamber. What is the qualitative consequence for chamber design?

options: Maximise tube diameter and minimise length; $S_{\rm eff}$ is limited by whichever is smaller — pump speed or conductance; a long narrow tube can be the bottleneck
Conductance grows linearly with tube length $L$, so longer tubes give faster pumping
Pump speed always equals the conductance in a properly designed system; the formula gives them equal weight
Conductance is pressure-dependent in molecular flow because the mean-free-path enters the formula
passive.explanation: Practical example: a turbo pump with $S = 300$ L/s connected via a 20 cm long, 4 cm diameter tube has $C = 280$ L/s, giving $S_{\rm eff} = 145$ L/s — half the pump speed is lost. Conversely, a 2 cm diameter tube 50 cm long has $C \approx 20$ L/s, making the effective speed pump-limited to 19 L/s regardless of pump size. Always maximise the diameter of tubes connecting pumps to chambers.
